title: Gibeon: Where the Sun Stood Still: The Discovery of the Biblical City
author: James B. Pritchard
allowedUseNotes: Copyrighted excavation synthesis. Project stores its own summary of the identification evidence only; no reproductions of photographs, drawings, or inscribed-handle transcriptions.
summary: Pritchard's synthesis of his University of Pennsylvania Museum excavations at el-Jib (Tell el-Jib), 1956-1962, reporting the site's Iron Age pool and rock-cut water-tunnel system and the jar handles recovered there inscribed with the Hebrew triliteral gb'n ('Gibeon'), first published individually in his 1959 monograph 'Hebrew Inscriptions and Stamps from Gibeon.' The inscribed handles are the epigraphic basis for identifying el-Jib with biblical Gibeon, including the pool referenced in 2 Samuel 2:13. Regarding the pool's own dating (the open question flagged in docs/design/gibeon-pool-brief.md): secondary summaries of Pritchard's excavation reports consistently describe his own conclusion as dating the cylindrical pool's cutting to the start of Iron Age I (variously reported as the late 12th/early 13th century BCE, or more loosely 'the 11th century BCE'), i.e., well before the early Iron IIA setting of 2 Samuel 2. The roughly 60 gb'n-inscribed jar handles themselves are separately dated (on paleographic grounds) to the 8th-7th centuries BCE and were recovered from the pool's fill/debris layers, not from a construction-phase context — they date the pool's later disuse/fill history and the site's eventual destruction (c. 600 BCE per Pritchard's own account), not the pool's original hewing. See tamburrini-2021-gibeon-pool for a modern reassessment that revises the pool's own cutting to the 10th century BCE rather than Iron I proper, while independently confirming the same basic answer to the open dating question: the rock-cut pool predates or is contemporary with the narrative's setting, not a later Iron IIB/C construction that postdates it.
confidenceNotes: Bibliographic details (title, publisher, year, handle count) assembled from secondary/tertiary summaries (Wikipedia 'Al Jib jar handles' article; Penn Museum Expedition Magazine pieces), not from direct inspection of Pritchard's original monographs. TO VERIFY: exact handle count across seasons (secondary sources give figures ranging from roughly 30 to 60+) and page-level citation once a copy of the 1959 or 1962 volume is consulted directly. The pool's own dating (added 2026-07-23, researcher pass): also assembled from secondary summaries only (a Wikipedia-sourced description and independent blog/reference-site paraphrases of the excavation reports), not Pritchard's original monograph text directly; the specific century figures reported for 'Pritchard's own dating' vary between sources (late 12th/13th century vs. 11th century BCE) in a way this project could not resolve to a single precise figure — both fall well before 2 Samuel 2's setting, which is the substantive point, but the exact figure is TO VERIFY against a primary copy.
